title: Vanilla fudge
subtitle: Creamy condensed milk fudge with a smooth, meltingly soft texture
excerpt: A classic condensed milk fudge cooked to the soft-ball stage, beaten off the heat until thick and creamy, and poured into a tin to set. Smooth, dense, and deeply sweet. Makes about 36 pieces.
sourceNotes: Condensed milk fudge is documented in British and American household confectionery guides from the late nineteenth century. The condensed milk formula, combining condensed milk, butter, and sugar cooked to the soft-ball stage, became the standard British home fudge recipe through the twentieth century, documented in household recipe books and Women's Institute publications. The technique of cooling then beating to develop smooth, small crystals distinguishes fudge from toffee and tablet.

content.text: Fudge texture comes from crystal size. Beating the cooked sugar mixture off the heat as it cools encourages many tiny crystals to form, giving the smooth, melt-in-the-mouth texture. Stop too early and it will be grainy; beat too long and it will set in the pan. The target is when the fudge goes from glossy to matte and begins to thicken. The
is the temperature to hit on the way up.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Combine the {{condensed-milk}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a heavy-based saucepan. Heat gently, stirring, until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil, stirring constantly to prevent catching. Cook at a steady boil, stirring, until the mixture reaches 115°C on a {{sugar-thermometer}}. Remove from the heat immediately. Stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}.

Leave the pan to cool undisturbed until the bottom of the pan is comfortably warm to the touch, about 10 minutes. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon or electric hand mixer until the mixture loses its gloss, thickens, and starts to hold its shape. This takes 3 to 5 minutes. Pour immediately into the prepared tin. Leave to set at room temperature for 2 hours, then cut into squares.
